scenario_results.reason: Normal in-scope cannabis reporting produced explicit metric definitions, source precedence, caveats, and action thresholds grounded in seed-to-sale, POS, finance, lab, and benchmark records.
Denominator ambiguity for sell-through and shrink was blocked until the tenant definition and source hierarchy were clarified.
Missing stores and stale traceability data triggered a low-confidence escalation instead of a smoothed cannabis trend claim.
Seed-to-sale, POS, and accounting disagreements were preserved as a reconciliation case with no single authoritative metric asserted.
The lane refused to turn price and sales movements into a causal claim and identified the evidence gaps needed for stronger inference.
Unsupported disclosure language was refused and routed to compliance, finance, legal, and disclosure owners with unresolved records named explicitly.
Missing jurisdiction, license class, system-of-record, and retrieval hooks produced a blocked-prerequisites response instead of implied comparability.
A hemp-adjacent retail analytics request was rejected cleanly so the cannabis label did not become cosmetic.
Remediation, regulator-reporting, and release authority were rejected cleanly and routed to the correct adjacent specialists.
Cannabis terminology was normalized to explicit package, batch, manifest, tax, and lab artifact shapes without collapsing into generic analytics language.
Tenant-specific metric adaptation was recorded explicitly while preserving the baseline definition and comparison posture.
Missing COA, remediation, and safety ownership context prevented unsupported trend or disclosure conclusions and forced escalation.

notes: All 12 scenarios passed against the standard 0.92 threshold while keeping the lane specific to cannabis metrics, seed-to-sale truth sets, testing dependencies, disclosure-safe reporting, and adjacent-lane escalation.
Functionality coverage is complete for all shared abilities, all analytics-reporting-and-disclosure abilities, and the cross-industry ability required by the audit contract.
